inline_review:
commit 94064e70c8770a2df75b8816bc9caa1582638f1e
Author: Waiman Long <longman@redhat.com>

selftests: memcg: Treat failure for zeroing sock in test_memcg_sock as XFAIL

This commit changes the test result of test_memcg_sock to an expected failure
(XFAIL) when the memory.stat sock counter fails to drop to zero within the
allotted 3 seconds. It aims to prevent intermittent CI failures caused by
the delay of asynchronous memcg rstat flushes.

> diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_memcontrol.c b/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_memcontrol.c
> --- a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_memcontrol.c
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_memcontrol.c
[ ... ]
> @@ -1477,12 +1477,20 @@ static int test_memcg_sock(const char *root)
>  	 * Poll memory.stat for up to 3 seconds (~FLUSH_TIME plus some
>  	 * scheduling slack) and require that the "sock " counter
>  	 * eventually drops to zero.
> +	 *
> +	 * The actual run-to-run elapse time between consecutive run
> +	 * of asynchronous memcg rstat flush may varies quite a bit.
> +	 * So the 3 seconds wait time may not be enough for the "sock"
> +	 * counter to go down to 0. Treat it as a XFAIL instead of
> +	 * a FAIL.
>  	 */
>  	sock_post = cg_read_key_long_poll(memcg, "memory.stat", "sock ", 0,
>  					 MEMCG_SOCKSTAT_WAIT_RETRIES,
>  					 DEFAULT_WAIT_INTERVAL_US);
> -	if (sock_post)
> +	if (sock_post) {
> +		ret = KSFT_XFAIL;
>  		goto cleanup;
> +	}

Does this code inadvertently mask actual system or I/O errors?

If cg_read_key_long_poll() returns -1 because it failed to read the file or
the key is missing, this check will treat it as an expected timeout failure
rather than an actual test failure.

Does marking this condition as KSFT_XFAIL prevent the test from catching
genuine socket memory leaks?

If a kernel regression causes socket memory to actually leak, the test will
time out and report an expected failure, which CI systems might ignore.

Would it be more robust to increase the polling timeout to accommodate the
maximum latency observed, or manually trigger a synchronous flush, instead
of masking the timeout?
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Read errors are incorrectly masked as expected test failures (XFAIL).","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"The function `cg_read_key_long_poll()` returns `-1` on errors, such as when it fails to read the file or if the specified key ('sock ') is missing from `memory.stat`. By changing the check to a generic `if (sock_post) { ret = KSFT_XFAIL; ... }`, the patch inadvertently causes actual system or I/O errors (`sock_post < 0`) to be reported as expected timeout failures (`KSFT_XFAIL`). This contradicts the author's intent to distinguish the timeout from other failure cases. The logic should explicitly differentiate between a timeout (`sock_post > 0`, which may be an `XFAIL`) and an actual read error (`sock_post < 0`, which should remain a `KSFT_FAIL`)."},{"problem":"Using KSFT_XFAIL for the 'sock' counter timeout effectively disables the test's ability to catch memory leak regressions.","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"By returning `KSFT_XFAIL` when the 'sock' memory counter fails to drop to zero, the test loses the ability to distinguish between a slow asynchronous rstat flush and a genuine kernel socket memory leak. If a kernel regression is introduced that actually leaks socket memory, the test will simply time out and report `KSFT_XFAIL` (Expected Failure), which is typically ignored by CI systems. This neuters the primary diagnostic value of the test. A more robust solution would be to increase the polling timeout (e.g., to accommodate the 10-15s maximum latency observed) or manually trigger a synchronous flush, rather than unconditionally masking the timeout as an expected failure."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 94064e70c8770a2df75b8816bc9caa1582638f1e

> --- a/tools/testing/selftests/cgroup/test_memcontrol.c

[ ... ]
> @@ -1477,12 +1477,20 @@ static int test_memcg_sock(const char *root)
>  	 * Poll memory.stat for up to 3 seconds (~FLUSH_TIME plus some
>  	 * scheduling slack) and require that the "sock " counter
>  	 * eventually drops to zero.
> +	 *
> +	 * The actual run-to-run elapse time between consecutive run
> +	 * of asynchronous memcg rstat flush may varies quite a bit.
> +	 * So the 3 seconds wait time may not be enough for the "sock"
> +	 * counter to go down to 0. Treat it as a XFAIL instead of
> +	 * a FAIL.
>  	 */
>  	sock_post = cg_read_key_long_poll(memcg, "memory.stat", "sock ", 0,
>  					 MEMCG_SOCKSTAT_WAIT_RETRIES,
>  					 DEFAULT_WAIT_INTERVAL_US);
> -	if (sock_post)
